query: efficacy of ofatumumab in multiple sclerosis

retrieved_docs.title: Ocrelizumab -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Ocrelizumab is an FDA-approved CD20-directed humanized monoclonal antibody indicated to treat adult patients with primary progressive or relapsing multiple sclerosis; it received approval from the FDA on March 28, 2017. FDA-approved indications (with clinical trial summaries):

retrieved_docs.title: Ocrelizumab -- Indications -- Primary-Progressive MS
retrieved_docs.content: ORATORIO study: In this double-blinded study, 732 patients were randomly assigned 2 to 1 to either ocrelizumab (two intravenous 300 mg doses given 14 days apart every 24 weeks) or placebo for at least 120 weeks until a prespecified number of disability events occurred. The study's primary endpoint was the percentage of patients with 12-week confirmed disability progression.  Ocrelizumab showed a 12-week confirmed disability progression of 32.9%, whereas the placebo arm showed a 39.3% rate (hazard ratio = 0.76, p=0.03). [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Cladribine -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Cladribine underwent several steps in regulatory approval before Merck-Serono released data from the Cladribine Tablets Treating Multiple Sclerosis Orally (CLARITY) trial in 2008. This study showed cladribine's gradual but steady acceptance in the European medical community as an effective and safe drug for multiple sclerosis. CLARITY was a randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial treating 1326 patients with relapsing multiple sclerosis (defined by revised McDonald criteria) to receive either placebo, lower-dose cladribine or higher-dose cladribine. The trial showed a 58% reduction in annualized relapse rates at 2 years with a lower dose of cladribine and a 55% reduction with a higher dose. The European Medicines Agency (EMA) granted cladribine final approval in August of 2017 for relapsing multiple sclerosis. In 2019, the US FDA approved oral cladribine for relapsing multiple sclerosis after several landmark studies (CLARITY extension, ORACLE MS, and ONWARD trials) supported the results of the original CLARITY trial. These studies demonstrated that cladribine reduced lesion activity on MRI, increased the percentage of relapse-free patients, and decreased disability progression by 30%. This topic summarizes cladribine's mechanism, administration, known adverse effects, monitoring, treatment regimen, and relevant clinical trials, and also discusses interprofessional coordination of care related to multiple sclerosis management. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Multiple Sclerosis -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with secondary progressive, progressive-relapsing, and primary progressive multiple sclerosis primarily experience neurodegenerative processes. Due to this, disease-modifying therapies show varying effectiveness, ranging from possible benefits to limited impact on disease progression. Typically, younger patients with a shorter duration of progression tend to benefit more from these therapies.

retrieved_docs.title: Ocrelizumab -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Ocrelizumab is a medication used in the management and treatment of primary progressive and relapsing multiple sclerosis. It is in the anti-CD20 monoclonal antibody class of medications. This activity describes the indications, action, and contraindications for ocrelizumab as a valuable agent in multiple sclerosis management. This activity will highlight the mechanism of action, adverse event profile, and other key factors (e.g., administration, contraindications, monitoring, toxicity) pertinent for interprofessional team members in the care of patients with multiple sclerosis.

retrieved_docs.title: Ocrelizumab -- Indications -- Relapsing MS
retrieved_docs.content: OPERA I study: In this double-blinded study, 821 patients were randomly assigned 1 to 1 to either ocrelizumab (two intravenous 300 mg doses given 14 days apart and then a single 600 mg doses every 24 weeks) or interferon beta-1a (subcutaneous 44 microgram dose three times weekly) for 96 weeks with a primary endpoint of annualized relapse rate. Results showed that ocrelizumab has a statistically significantly lower annualized relapse rate at 0.16 vs. interferon beta-1a at 0.29 (46% reduction, p<0.001). [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Cladribine -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Oral Cladribine in Early Multiple Sclerosis (ORACLE MS) was another multi-center, double-blind, randomized, phase III study of oral cladribine which showed a significant risk reduction compared to placebo for time to conversion to clinically definite multiple sclerosis, as measured by magnetic resonance imaging (hazard ratio [HR] for 5.25 mg/kg = 0.38, 95% CI 0.25 - 0.58, p<0.0001; HR for 3.5 mg/kg = 0.33, 95% CI 0.21 - 0.51, p<0.0001), for adults who had experienced a first clinically-isolated demyelinating syndrome (CIS). [22] Investigators stopped the ORACLE MS trial early because 5% of patients in the high-dose cladribine group and 2% in the low-dose cladribine group developed severe lymphopenia. A follow-up reanalysis of the ORACLE MS data using modern multiple sclerosis diagnostic criteria was published in 2017 and upheld cladribine’s efficacy in early multiple sclerosis. Stuve and colleagues demonstrated that cladribine-induced lymphopenia is reversible and that the T-cell and B-cell populations can reconstitute. [16] More recently, a phase II, multicenter, randomized study of add-on cladribine with interferon-beta (the ONWARD trial) found that 124 participants who were randomly assigned to receive cladribine 3.5 mg/kg plus IFN-ß (N=124) were 63% less likely to have a multiple sclerosis relapse compared with participants assigned to the placebo plus IFN-ß group ( p < 0.001). Cladribine also reduced the mean number of new T1 and T2 MRI lesions in the combined treatment arm compared to placebo plus IFN-ß. [23] [24]

retrieved_docs.title: Natalizumab -- Indications -- FDA-Approved Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Natalizumab has been shown to slow down the progression of symptoms during a flare-up in individuals with multiple sclerosis and decrease the rate of relapse in those with relapsing-remitting multiple sclerosis. In a randomized, placebo-controlled study, 77% of patients were relapse-free at the end of 1 year compared to 56% of those on placebo. The 2-year relapse-free rate was 67% with natalizumab versus 41% with placebo. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Multiple Sclerosis -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Natalizumab: This drug is an intravenously (IV) administered humanized monoclonal antibody that blocks leukocyte adhesion to vascular endothelial cells, thereby inhibiting leukocyte migration into the CNS. Although generally well tolerated, natalizumab may cause mild headaches, and flushing may occur during IV administration.

retrieved_docs.title: Cladribine -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Multiple sclerosis is an immune-mediated inflammatory demyelinating disease of the central nervous system that results in loss of oligodendrocytes, astroglial scarring, and axonal injury. Certain clinical features are typical of multiple sclerosis, but the disease has a highly variable presentation. Neurological symptoms present through time and space, making it a complex disorder with atypical forms. Major subtypes include relapsing multiple sclerosis, secondary progressive multiple sclerosis, and primary progressive multiple sclerosis. [1] [2] Relapsing multiple sclerosis is the most common form, characterized by cycles of relapses (exacerbations with a sustained worsening of demyelinating symptoms compared to baseline) and remissions (no clinical evidence of disease activity), with variable frequency and duration. Secondary progressive multiple sclerosis presents as a steady decline in neurological function. [3] [4] Primary progressive multiple sclerosis is less common and presents with an insidious onset of demyelinating symptoms followed by steady symptom progression with no discrete relapses. This activity considers cladribine, an oral drug recently approved by the FDA for relapsing multiple sclerosis. [5] [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Cladribine -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: In the late 20th century, the discovery of cladribine led to a long journey to FDA approval. As an oral medication given once a year as a maintenance dose, cladribine is an attractive option for multiple sclerosis disease-modifying therapy with important implications for patient experience, enhanced quality of life outcomes in different patient populations, and reduced health care costs. Several studies have shown that cladribine can improve healthcare outcomes for adults living with multiple sclerosis. However, one must still address the role of comprehensive multiple sclerosis care centers in administering complex immunosuppressive medications and other aspects of multiple sclerosis care.

retrieved_docs.title: Natalizumab -- Indications -- FDA-Approved Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Natalizumab is an FDA-approved monoclonal antibody for treating multiple sclerosis and Crohn disease (CD). [1] The drug was initially approved to treat multiple sclerosis in 2004, but after reported mortality cases due to progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy associated with natalizumab treatment, the FDA removed the drug from the market. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Immunotherapy -- Mechanism of Action -- Intravenous Immunoglobulins (IVIG)
retrieved_docs.content: Multiple sclerosis

retrieved_docs.title: Cladribine -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: The original CLARITY placebo group, however, entered CLARITY Extension with a higher mean EDSS score, reflecting an increased disability level that followed these study participants through the CLARITY Extension monitoring period, as manifested by higher mean ARR ( p <0.0001) and shorter time to first relapse compared with those CLARITY participants who had received either dose of cladribine. [17] [18] More recently, a posthoc analysis of CLARITY data found that 3.5 mg/kg of cladribine tablets reduced MRI markers of disease activity in patients with highly active relapsing multiple sclerosis. Cladribine conferred a 47% risk reduction for worsening EDSS scores 6 months post-treatment compared to placebo. A published subgroup analysis found that patients with the highest level of multiple sclerosis disease activity experienced the same or greater risk reduction (82% compared to placebo) compared with the rest of the CLARITY cohort, with a comparable safety profile. [19] [20] [21]

retrieved_docs.title: Ocrelizumab -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Ocrelizumab is a second-generation anti-CD20 recombinant monoclonal antibody with a molecular mass of 145kDa. It is a follow-up to the anti-CD20 monoclonal antibody rituximab but with a humanized IgG1 tail. [3] Humanization of the antibody is expected to reduce antigenicity and improve CD20 binding. [4] CD20 is a phosphoprotein expressed on a broad range of human B-cells. However, it is not present in the early lineage (stem cells and pro-B-cells) or late lineage (plasmablasts and plasma cells). [5] Ocrelizumab binds to CD20 on B-lymphocytes leading to antibody-dependent cellular cytolysis and complement-mediated lysis. [6] While the precise method by which ocrelizumab exerts its effect on multiple sclerosis is unknown, it depletes B-cells which are thought to affect the pathogenesis of multiple sclerosis using cytokine regulation, antigen presentation, autoantibody production. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Cladribine -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Together, these data from the oral cladribine trials show an impressive potential to improve a range of healthcare outcomes for adults with multiple sclerosis. Potential concerns regarding the use of cladribine include patient safety issues, such as lymphopenia, and the need for extended monitoring for the effects of prolonged immunosuppression. For these reasons, the manufacturer recommends that clinicians reserve cladribine for adults who had an inadequate response to or did not tolerate other multiple sclerosis disease-modifying therapies. Recommendations may include referring patients who are potential candidates for cladribine to a comprehensive multiple sclerosis care center when possible. A comprehensive multiple sclerosis care center, or multiple sclerosis unit, is a specialized program that offers an interprofessional and patient-centered approach to diagnosing, treating, and managing multiple sclerosis. Comprehensive multiple sclerosis care centers offer a cross-disciplinary team approach to delivering state-of-the-art multiple sclerosis disease-modifying therapy and symptom management while serving as centers for multiple sclerosis research.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1239
retrieved_docs.content: Triamcinolone, a corticosteroid with anti‑inflammatory properties, may be used in the treatment of multiple sclerosis (التصلب اللويحي), highlighting its role in managing inflammatory and autoimmune neurological conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 2 -- Community 308
retrieved_docs.content: Cykl​ofosfamid, an alkylating chemotherapeutic and immunosuppressive agent, may be used to treat both multiple sclerosis and multiple myeloma, illustrating its therapeutic relevance across a demyelinating autoimmune disorder and a hematologic malignancy. Its capacity to modulate immune activity and inhibit proliferating cells underlies its application in these distinct diseases, emphasizing the importance of versatile treatment options in personalized patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 3 -- Community 2467
retrieved_docs.content: Triamcinolone, identified as “triamcinolon,” is classified within the group of moderately potent corticosteroids that are used in various combination formulations (“Corticosteroids, moderately potent, other combinations”). As a member of this corticosteroid class, triamcinolone possesses anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive properties that make it a therapeutic option for managing multiple sclerosis (التصلب اللويحي). By recognizing triamcinolone’s place among moderately potent corticosteroids, clinicians can better understand its mechanism of action, appropriate dosing, and potential side‑effect profile when it is employed to reduce inflammation and disease activity in patients with multiple sclerosis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 1471
retrieved_docs.content: التصلب اللويحي (multiple sclerosis) may be treated with triamcinolon, a drug that belongs to the class of systemic glucocorticoids. Systemic glucocorticoids, represented by both triamcinolon and rimexolone, share anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive properties and are employed in the management of various inflammatory and autoimmune disorders. Recognizing that triamcinolon and rimexolone are members of the systemic glucocorticoid group informs clinicians in selecting appropriate steroid therapy for conditions such as multiple sclerosis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1600
retrieved_docs.content: Triamcinolone is a corticosteroid that may be employed in the treatment of multiple sclerosis (التصلب اللويحي). However, its use is contraindicated in the setting of pregnancy (حالَةٌ رَبْوِيَّة). Clinicians should therefore balance the potential benefit of triamcinolone for managing multiple sclerosis against the risk of adverse effects in pregnant patients and consider alternative therapies when a pregnancy is present.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 1990
retrieved_docs.content: Vitamin B12 may be used to treat multiple sclerosis, indicating a potential therapeutic role for this vitamin in managing the neurological disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 3 -- Community 1298
retrieved_docs.content: Dantrolen and vitamin B12 are both identified as potential therapeutic agents for multiple sclerosis, suggesting that each may be used to help manage this demyelinating neurological disorder. Their inclusion highlights the exploration of diverse treatment options in the care of patients with multiple sclerosis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 1490
retrieved_docs.content: Methylprednisolone, a corticosteroid medication, may be used to treat multiple sclerosis, offering anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive effects that can help reduce disease activity. In addition, vitamin B12 is also considered a potential therapeutic agent for multiple sclerosis, as supplementation may support neurological function and address deficiencies that can exacerbate symptoms. Both methylprednisolone and vitamin B12 represent possible treatment options in the management of multiple sclerosis, highlighting the importance of a multifaceted therapeutic approach.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 2159
retrieved_docs.content: Triamcinolon is identified as a therapeutic option that may be used to treat التصلب اللويحي (multiple sclerosis). It is classified within the group of Corticosteroids, moderately potent, other combinations, indicating that it belongs to a class of moderately potent corticosteroids that can be combined with other agents. Additionally, Triamcinolon is a member of the category Corticosteroids for local oral treatment, highlighting its formulation for localized oral application. Together, these relationships describe Triamcinolon as a moderately potent corticosteroid formulated for local oral use and potentially applicable in the management of multiple sclerosis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 4 -- Community 2915
retrieved_docs.content: Triamcinolone, identified as a corticosteroid formulated for local oral treatment, is classified within the group of corticosteroids used for local oral administration. This medication may be employed in the therapeutic management of multiple sclerosis (التصلب اللويحي). The association of triamcinolone with the class “Corticosteroids for local oral treatment” underscores its role as a locally administered anti‑inflammatory agent, while its potential use in multiple sclerosis highlights the broader application of corticosteroids in treating inflammatory demyelinating disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1741
retrieved_docs.content: Triamcinolone is a moderately potent corticosteroid (group II) that may be used in the treatment of multiple sclerosis (التصلب اللويحي). Alclometasone also belongs to the same class of moderately potent corticosteroids (group II). Both triamcinolone and alclometasone are members of the corticosteroid, moderately potent (group II) category, sharing similar anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive properties. Recognizing that these agents are part of the same corticosteroid subgroup assists clinicians in selecting appropriate anti‑inflammatory therapy for conditions such as multiple sclerosis and other inflammatory disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1823
retrieved_docs.content: Human corticotropin and several corticosteroid formulations—including methylprednisolon acetate, methylprednisolone aceponate, and methylprednisolone sodium phosphate—are identified as potential therapeutic agents for multiple sclerosis. These drugs act by modulating immune activity and reducing inflammatory processes that contribute to demyelination and neurological deficits in multiple sclerosis. Their use, particularly during acute relapses, may help alleviate symptoms, limit lesion formation, and improve short‑term neurological function, highlighting the role of corticosteroid‑based therapies in the management of this chronic autoimmune disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1530
retrieved_docs.content: Human corticotropin, methylprednisolon acetát, and methylprednisolone sodium phosphate are all identified as agents that may be used to treat multiple sclerosis. These therapies, which include the peptide hormone ACTH (corticotropin) and two corticosteroid formulations, are employed to modulate the immune response and reduce inflammatory activity that underlies the demyelinating process of multiple sclerosis. Their potential use reflects a therapeutic approach aimed at controlling disease activity and improving neurological outcomes in patients with this chronic autoimmune disorder.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 3 -- Community 2553
retrieved_docs.content: Triamcinolone is a corticosteroid that belongs to the moderately potent (group II) class of corticosteroids and is formulated for local oral treatment. It is recognized as a therapeutic option that may be used in the management of multiple sclerosis (التصلب اللويحي). Alclometasone is likewise a member of the moderately potent (group II) corticosteroid group. Both triamcinolone and alclometasone share the same potency classification within the broader corticosteroid category, which guides clinicians in selecting appropriate anti‑inflammatory agents for various conditions, including the potential use of triamcinolone in multiple sclerosis therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2293
retrieved_docs.content: Human corticotropin and the corticosteroid formulations methylprednisolone aceponate and methylprednisolone sodium phosphate are all identified as agents that may be used to treat multiple sclerosis, highlighting the potential role of corticosteroid‑based therapies in managing this demyelinating disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 1491
retrieved_docs.content: Human corticotropin, methylprednisolon acetate, and methylprednisolone aceponate are all identified as agents that may be used to treat multiple sclerosis, indicating that several corticosteroid‑related medications are considered potential therapeutic options for managing this demyelinating disease.
